summary: Peak‑era set with soaring Dark Star > Morning Dew and tight jams
blurb: The Berkeley show delivers a soaring Dark Star‑Morning Dew sequence, crisp guitar work from Jerry and solid rhythm throughout. Highlights include Bird Song, Truckin’, Tennessee Jed and an uplifting Uncle John’s Band encore.
review: On August 24, 1972 at the Berkeley Community Theater the Dead hit a stride that still feels fresh decades later. The first set rolls through classic cuts—The Promised Land, Sugaree, China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider—and builds momentum with an especially spirited Bird Song and tight Tennessee Jed. The second set ignites with Mississippi Half‑Step before launching into a blistering Dark Star that morphs into a soaring Morning Dew, followed by a joyous Sugar Magnolia, Ramble On Rose, and the rousing Greatest Story Ever Told. The night closes on a warm Uncle John’s Band encore, leaving the audience buzzing. Jerry’s half‑step opener and intense tone in Dark Star drive the performance, Phil’s bass is inventive throughout, Bob’s rhythm guitar shines on Mississippi Half‑Step, and Keith’s keyboards add tasteful texture without ever overpowering the mix.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings note strong, standout moments (Dark Star>Morning Dew, Bird Song, Truckin', Tennessee Jed) and only one mentions minor weak spots; no consistent negative commentary on performance, yielding a solid positive sentiment across the set.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard mix that captures the soaring Dark Star→Morning Dew and overall set flow with no dropouts, delivering the most vivid listening experience.

key_highlights: Soaring Dark Star → Morning Dew sequence as the second‑set centerpiece
Tight Truckin' and uplifting Tennessee Jed that showcase tight band interplay
Bird Song stands out as a highlight of the first set
Excellent setlist flow culminating in an upbeat Uncle John's Band encore

band_performance.Jerry: Rollicking half‑step opener and intense guitar tone in Dark Star drive the night’s energy, with a sparky but occasionally faltered solo on Jack Straw.
band_performance.Phil: In top form—boop‑y solo on Sugaree, inventive bass lines (early Elastic Jazz riff) and strong harmonic support throughout.
band_performance.Bob: Solid rhythm guitar and vocals; ES‑335 tone shines on Mississippi Half‑Step and Brown Eyed Women.
